THE. ADVENTURES OF SUIBHNE GEILT.

(BUILE SUIBHNIC.)

&  Mr. J. G. O’Keeffe’s admirable edition of the above work forms the
: twelfth volume of the publications of the Irish Texts Society. The
frenzv of Suibhne, king of the northern territory of Dal Ar aidhe, 1z one
of the many evils ascribed in Irish mediseval hiterature as arising out of
ﬂle curse of some famous saint. The young prince is annoyed that St.
“ % Ronan Finn, Abbot of Drumiskin, Co. Louth, should determine to
set’ole himself down upon his territory. In his anger he siezes the
" saint’s psalter and flings it 1nto the lake. Just as he is endeavouring
| fOI‘ClblV to eject St. Ronan a messenger arrives to bid Suibhne join the
Kmg of Uladh Congal Claen, at his hosting at Magh Rath, or Moira,
an historical battle known hoth to the Irish and Scottish annals and

mntloned in Adamnan’s Life of St. Columba. As he goes, the saint

cursns him, praying that he ‘‘be ever wandering and flying stark

llaked through the world. That his death be from a spear-point.”” All
;;’ ﬂllﬁ comes -to pass. Like the maledictions of their predecessors the
f ‘Druidg, or bards, whose powers they were popularly supposed to have

mhcnted the curse of a saint was followed by the doom of the person

Fho had given offence. At the hattle of Maghi Rath the din and horror

Wel‘e go great that the young prince is driven
ﬂles from the scene and thenceforward runt
Bpea.r he wanders, half leaping, half flyi
ﬂle branches of trees at night, and as

ﬁ"J‘vlng water cresses to support life. time
" Was faring throughout Troland, visiting and goarching in hard, rocky

"leffﬂ and in bushy branches of tall ivy trees, in NArrow cavitios of
" stones, from estuary to cstuary, trom peak te peak, and from glen to

when their fear of madness was complete, that glen being ever a plage % cL Fo
of great delight to madmen.” 1t seems to be the corresponding hiding. o [N
place in the north of Ireland to *‘ Gleann na nGealt,”” or the ‘‘ Glen of i g
the Watercresses,” as it is called in modern folk lore, in Co. Kerry. iir wh
Mr. O’Keeffe suggests an identification of Glen Bolcain with Drum | ad
Boleain, in Co. Antrim, though its mention 1n the tale of the Battle of - an

Ventry suggests some district in the south of Ireland. In spite of the -.-*__th:
curious character of Suibhne’s adventures, there is much in the rom-
ance to attract the attention of the literary reader as well as the folk-
lorist. It is interspersed with long poems, showing a dclicate and
beautiful observation of mature; they compare favourably with the
finest. of the Ossianic nature-poems. The language of the prose is
terse and has little resemblance, except in a few short passages, to ,;“:11<

r the redundant prose of the Battle of Magh Rath with which, both 8
| historically and also in the manuscripts in which it is found, 1% is A b
':i otherwise closely associated; forming, as the Editor suggests, a small - §
H' . story-cyele with it and the Banquet of Dun nman Gedh, Mr, O’Keefte

has taken as the foundation of his text the same manuscript as that &
used by Dr. O’Donovan for his edition of the Battle of Magh Rath,
published in 1842 for the Irish Avchaological Socicty, and has com- e (
pared it with a copy found among the Stowe MSS. in the R.I. Academy,
and with what appears to he & summary of the tale from a MS. in the &
Royal Library, Bruseels. Though these are late texts, the story itself &
is ancient. Mr. O’Keeffe points to a reference to it in the Irish law |
tract, entitled the Book of Aicill, and also from the mention of it in .
== the Norse account of Trish wonders in the °‘ Speculum Regale,”” a book 3
. written about 1250 AD. We do not think, however, with ﬂié‘-’r“, 4

o TWO NEW IRISH NOVELISTS.

.; Amidst the countless volumes of fiction that have poured forth
i ~ from the press this past month, two stand out conspicuously as being
3_" | not only far and away ahove the avérage of such works, but as being

love entanglement in which that fine fellow Ifitz gerald—alwaysg ready

in any ('mcrrrency-—cﬂn alrously plunges, is skillfully handled, and p;s. |

The second, FATHER RaLrm, by Gerald O’Donovan (Macmillan) deals {

with a far different atmosphere and sct of characters. It is an |

unusually interesting tale. It is the story of the slow making, and the ;:'

speedy unmaking, of an Irish priest. Written with evident inside 4’f::. |

| knowledge of the Irish Church, it is severe, but 1t 1s at the same 9

: time dispassionate, and just, and scrupulously reverent towards the |

| essentials of religion. Every incident in the book is thoroughly char- ==

acteristic of the people 1t depicts. If there is a little harshness in the |

S picture of seminary life, there is no exaggeration, and the Maynooth

3 scenes aroe rofreshingly true to life. We are glad to say that te

ot 2 venomous enemies of the Catholic Church the story will be disappoint-

H_ ‘ing: But to all, of whaterer sect, “who practise the Christianity they
L = believe in, the book will have a strong appeal. , :

GOSSIP.

The fame of Henry James as a novelist is wide spread on both sides
of the Atlantic. It is therefore interesting to learn from his recent
Autobrographical work ‘A Small Boy and Others,” that like President ‘
Wilson, Edear Allen Pog, and many other American celebrities he: )
tracos hic descent from good Ulster stock. His grandfather, William
James, was born in Co. Cavam, and emigrated to America at the close
- of the war of Independence, and his maternal great-grandfather, Hugh
i Walsh, of Killyleagh, in Co. Down, landed in Amecrica in 1764, &

For ncarly half a century John Wilson Croker bhore an unenviable |

reputation for hatred, malice and all mncharitableness as a lterary
_critic. Tady Morgamn, ‘Disracli, Thackeray and Macaulay had all, by
turns, held him wp to scorn, and it was not untal neavly twenty years
st  after his deathithat amy attempt was made to show that the devil was |
not go black as ho was painted. This was in aw anticle in the “ Quarterly  #
Review ”’ for July, 1876, provoked by the plnbhcatlcn of the Life of |
Lord Macaulay by his nephew, the present Sir George Trevelyan, which
contained several aspersiong on Croker.

The name of the writer of the articla, who evidently had the family
papers placed at his disposal, has always remained a secret, but 16 §0°
no longer. (m weply to a query in a recent number of that never fa-ili‘lig-‘ B
source of information, ‘‘Notes and Queries,”” Mr. John Murray, the '_
present head of the famous publishing house in Albemarle Street, had : B
the kindness to write as follows: ¢ Withi reference to the query of the .'
Fditor of ‘ The Irish Book Lover,” concerning the anticle on Jokn Wﬂ" .5
son Croker, which appeared in The Quarterly Review of J uly, 1876, I
bhrmk there is no reason why I should not now stiate publicly that it was f
- written by the lato Sir William Smith, who was at that time editor O_gf ;.l )
‘;H ~ The Quarterly.”” And so amother litblo literary mystery ll.a-s b i
~ cleared up throughi the agenoy of I.B.L. - - ?4"‘;;‘
